blocks.text: Chapter 4.
Reality-Test: Consider the Opposite
The ideas in 30 seconds

People gather evidence like defense attorneys, not judges: a meta-analysis by Hart and colleagues covering 91 studies and more than 8,000 participants found we are over twice as likely to seek information that confirms our existing beliefs as information that challenges them.

Confirmation bias hides in the question we silently ask ourselves — "Can I believe this?" for conclusions we like, "Must I believe this?" for conclusions we don't (Gilovich) — so collecting more data will not fix a rigged search.

Because you cannot simply will the bias away, engineer disagreement structurally: appoint a devil's advocate, run a murder board, or, like Alfred Sloan, postpone a unanimous decision until dissent has had time to develop.

Disconfirming questions extract truth that polite ones never will: in the iPod-sale experiment, asking "What problems does it have?" led 89% of sellers to disclose a hidden defect, versus 8% for a generic opening question.
Experts fight their own first impressions with anti-anchoring habits — diagnosticians deliberately ask "What else could this be?" and "What doesn't fit?" before committing to an answer.

When positions are entrenched, stop asking people what they believe and ask, with Roger Martin, what would have to be true for each option to be the right one — a move that turns adversaries into joint investigators.
Your Mind Argues Like a Lawyer, Not a Judge

When we favor a conclusion, we don't evaluate evidence — we recruit it. Confirmation bias is the second villain of decision making: we develop a quick belief, then send our attention out to gather support for it, sincerely convinced we are being objective the whole time.

The scale of the distortion is well documented. A 2009 meta-analysis led by William Hart pooled 91 studies with more than 8,000 participants and found that people are more than twice as likely to seek out confirming information as disconfirming information. The skew gets worse exactly when it matters most: it intensifies in emotionally charged domains like religion and politics, and when people have already invested time, effort, or identity in their position.

Psychologist Tom Gilovich exposed the mechanism in the questions we pose to ourselves. When we want to believe something, we ask "Can I believe this?" — and a single supportive scrap of evidence gives us permission to say yes. When we don't want to believe something, we ask "Must I believe this?" — and a single flaw gives us permission to dismiss it. Same evidence, opposite verdicts, depending only on which standard of proof we quietly applied.

This is why intelligence and diligence don't protect you. Smart people don't escape confirmation bias; they build more sophisticated one-sided briefs. Piling up analysis inside a biased search process just produces a taller stack of agreeable data. Chapter 1's finding that decision process beats analysis six to one is largely about this: the fix is not thinking harder about the evidence you have, but changing how evidence gets collected in the first place. That means two moves this chapter develops — deliberately creating disagreement, and deliberately asking questions designed to prove yourself wrong.
Engineer Disagreement — Don't Wait for It

If individuals can't debias themselves, institutions can build dissent into the process. The Catholic Church did it for centuries. Starting in 1587 under Pope Sixtus V, every candidate for sainthood faced the devil's advocate (formally the "promoter of the faith"), a canon lawyer whose official job was to argue against canonization — probing character flaws and poking holes in miracle evidence. The role licensed skepticism: the advocate wasn't a troublemaker, he was doing his job. When John Paul II effectively abolished the office in 1983, canonizations exploded — he named more saints than his predecessors of the preceding several centuries combined. Remove the institutional skeptic, and the approval rate tells you what happens to scrutiny.

General Motors chairman Alfred Sloan applied the same logic to business. When a committee reached quick unanimous agreement on an important decision, Sloan proposed adjourning and taking the matter up at the next meeting — deliberately postponing so members had time to develop disagreement and actually understand what they were deciding. For Sloan, instant consensus wasn't a green light; it was evidence that nobody had yet reality-tested the idea.

The military's version is the murder board: a panel of experts whose explicit assignment is to kill a proposed project or shred a candidate's arguments in rehearsal — better to have a flawed plan die in a conference room than in the field. The same format preps nominees for hostile confirmation hearings, and companies can borrow it for major launches and investments: convene a group whose mandate, and only mandate, is to make the case for why this will fail.

Two design notes make engineered dissent work. First, protect the dissenter — criticism must be a sanctioned role, not a career risk, or you'll only ever hear it from people willing to burn political capital. Second, you don't need heavy machinery: rotating the devil's-advocate role, or simply seeking out your organization's existing skeptics and giving them cover to argue, captures most of the benefit.
Ask Questions That Could Hurt You

The second tool is aiming your own questions at your assumptions' weak points. Disconfirming questions presume that problems exist and ask what they are — and they dramatically outperform polite, open-ended ones when the other side knows something you don't.

Researchers Julia Minson, Nicole Ruedy, and Maurice Schweitzer demonstrated this with a role-play in which participants sold a used iPod that had twice frozen and wiped all its music. What buyers learned depended almost entirely on phrasing. A general opener — essentially "Tell me about it" — prompted only 8% of sellers to volunteer the defect. A positive-assumption question — "It doesn't have any problems, does it?" — raised disclosure to 61%. A negative-assumption, disconfirming question — "What problems does it have?" — pushed it to 89%. Sellers rarely lied outright; they simply let comfortable questions go unpunished. The disconfirming buyer signaled that evasion wouldn't work.

Use this framing wherever information is asymmetric and interests aren't aligned: buying a used car or a business, negotiating a lease, checking references, evaluating a vendor. Ask what problems the product has, why the last person in the role left, what customers complain about most — not whether everything is fine.

One caveat: aggressive probing fits adversarial settings, not every setting. With people who are inclined to tell you what you want to hear — employees, patients, anyone facing a power gradient — hard leading questions can intimidate rather than reveal, and open-ended prompts draw out more truth. And within a team, the goal is to disagree without being disagreeable; disconfirming questions should read as rigor, not hostility, or people will stop surfacing bad news at all. The constant is the intent: ask the question whose answer could genuinely change your mind, not the question that flatters your plan.
Break the Anchor: The Diagnostician's Habits

Confirmation bias has a fast-acting cousin: anchoring on the first plausible answer. Medicine shows both the danger and the countermeasures. A physician who forms an early hunch — this chest pain is reflux, this fatigue is stress — starts unconsciously collecting the symptoms that fit and discounting the ones that don't, a failure mode clinicians call premature closure. Since the first guess arrives within moments of meeting a patient, everything afterward risks becoming a can-I-believe-this exercise.

The medical profession's answer, popularized in Jerome Groopman's How Doctors Think, is a set of trained, almost ritualized disconfirming questions that good diagnosticians run against their own conclusions: What else could this be? Is there anything about this case that doesn't fit my diagnosis? Could more than one problem be going on at once? Some clinicians add a stakes-based check — what's the worst thing this could possibly be, and have I ruled it out? — so that a dangerous possibility gets eliminated deliberately rather than ignored comfortably.

Notice what these habits do: they treat the first explanation as a candidate rather than a verdict, and they force a search for the anomaly — the data point the favored theory can't explain. That's transferable far beyond medicine. When you've concluded that sales dipped because of seasonality, that a star candidate is the obvious hire, or that a project slipped because one team underperformed, run the same protocol. What else could explain this? What evidence doesn't fit? Am I collapsing two problems into one? The questions cost seconds; anchoring errors cost far more. The discipline isn't pessimism — it's making your first idea earn the confidence you were about to hand it for free.
When Debate Deadlocks: What Would Have to Be True?

Sometimes the problem isn't too little disagreement but too much of the wrong kind — camps dug in, each running its own confirmation-biased search for ammunition. Roger Martin, later dean of the Rotman School of Management, hit exactly that as a young consultant facilitating a strategy fight at a mining company wrestling with a failing copper mine. Asking the hostile room what they believed the company should do would have ignited a war of assertions. Instead he changed the question: for each option on the table, what would have to be true for this to be the best choice?

The reframe is subtly powerful. Nobody is asked to concede anything or defend a position, so the belief-protection machinery never engages. Advocates of an option must spell out the conditions under which it wins — market prices, cost levels, competitor behavior — turning a vague conviction into a testable checklist. Skeptics, meanwhile, articulate what evidence would convert them, instead of just sniping. The group then goes and checks which conditions actually hold, and the analysis, not the loudest voice, eliminates options. Martin considers it the most valuable question in his strategy toolkit: it converts advocates into analysts and arguments into research plans.

The consider-the-opposite move works on private judgments too, including the cynical stories we tell about other people. PepsiCo CEO Indra Nooyi called the best advice she ever received "assume positive intent": when someone's behavior irritates you, deliberately test the charitable interpretation instead of prosecuting the hostile one. Often the generous reading fits the facts at least as well — and responding to it produces better outcomes even when you were partly right to be annoyed.

Reality-testing, then, is one discipline in four forms: notice whether you're asking "can I" or "must I" believe; build structures that license dissent; ask questions engineered to disconfirm; and when conflict stalls, specify what would have to be true. Each one points your evidence-gathering at the spot where you're most likely to be wrong.
In practice

Before finalizing a significant decision this week, spend fifteen minutes searching only for disconfirming evidence — articles, data, or people that argue you're wrong — and write down the strongest case against your plan.

Assign a rotating devil's advocate for your team's next big proposal, with an explicit mandate (and explicit safety) to argue why it will fail; or convene a one-hour murder board before any major commitment.
If a group agrees instantly on something important, do what Sloan did: table it until the next meeting and task everyone with bringing one objection.

Swap polite openers for disconfirming questions in your next negotiation, reference check, or purchase: ask "What problems does it have?" and "Why did the last person leave?" — never "Everything's fine, right?"

When you settle on an explanation for anything — a metric's dip, a colleague's behavior, a diagnosis — force the diagnostician's checklist: What else could this be? What doesn't fit? Could two things be going on?
In your next deadlocked debate, go option by option and ask "What would have to be true for this to be the best choice?" — then assign someone to check whether those conditions actually hold.
